0:0.note: This bare list of ten names from Adam to Noah quietly echoes the ten generations from Adam to Noah in Genesis 5, pulling the whole primeval story into the Chronicler's family tree.

0:1.note: This tight genealogy compresses ten generations from Adam to Noah, echoing the rhythm of Genesis 5 while inviting readers to trace how the fractured human family still carries the promise of a coming seed.

0:2.note: This tight genealogy compresses ten generations from Adam to Noah into one list, echoing the same names in Genesis 5 yet stripping away every story to highlight the unbroken line of promise.

0:3.note: These four names launch the entire biblical story of the nations, each one becoming the ancestor of a major branch of humanity.

0:4.rendering: The sons of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras.
0:4.note: This list echoes the table of nations in Genesis 10, tracing the ancient world's family tree from Noah's three sons.

0:5.rendering: The sons of Gomer: Ashkenaz, Diphath, and Togarmah.
0:5.note: These three names link Gomer's line to ancient peoples on the edges of Israel's known world, echoing the table of nations in Genesis 10.

0:6.rendering: The sons of Javan: Elishah, Tarshish, the Kittim, and the Rodanim.
0:6.note: This list of Javan's sons echoes Genesis 10:4 but swaps Dodanim for Rodanim, a subtle ancient scribal variant pointing to the island of Rhodes.

0:7.rendering: The sons of Ham: Cush, Mizraim, Put, and Canaan.
0:7.note: This genealogy of the nations traces back to Noah's three sons, sketching the ancient world's family tree with Ham's line populating Africa and the eastern Mediterranean.

1:0.rendering: These are the sons of Israel: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun,
1:0.note: This list opens the Chronicler's genealogy by naming the twelve tribes from one father, echoing the opening of Genesis to show Israel's story as a continuation of God's ancient purposes.

1:1.rendering: Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher.
1:1.note: These six names complete the list of Jacob's twelve sons, echoing the tribal foundations laid in Genesis 29-30 and 35.

1:2.rendering: The sons of Judah were Er, Onan, and Shelah. These three were born to him by Bath-shua the Canaanite. But Er, Judah's firstborn, was evil in the eyes of the LORD, so he put him to death.
1:2.note: This opening of Judah's genealogy quietly echoes Genesis 38, where the same three sons and tragic deaths introduce the line that will eventually lead to David and beyond.

1:3.rendering: Judah's sons were Er, Onan, Shelah. These three were born to him by Bath-shua the Canaanite. Er, Judah's firstborn, was wicked in the LORD's sight, so he put him to death.
1:3.note: The shocking opening of Judah's family line echoes Genesis 38, where the Canaanite woman's sons reveal both human failure and the surprising thread of God's promise continuing despite wickedness.

1:4.rendering: The sons of Perez were Hezron and Hamul.
1:4.note: This terse genealogy quietly carries the seed of Judah's royal line, since Hezron will father the family from which David and the Messiah eventually spring.

1:5.rendering: The sons of Zerah: Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Dara, five in all.
1:5.note: These five names echo the wise men of 1 Kings 4:31, linking Zerah's line to legendary wisdom in Israel's memory.

1:6.rendering: The sons of Carmi: Achar, the troubler of Israel, who broke faith in the matter of the devoted thing.
1:6.note: Achar's name means "trouble," a deliberate wordplay echoing the disaster he brought on Israel by taking devoted plunder at Jericho.

1:7.note: This single-line entry quietly links the obscure Judahite line of Ethan to King Azariah, whose name echoes the famous wise king Uzziah later in the same tribe.

0:8.rendering: The sons of Cush were Seba, Havilah, Sabta, Raama, and Sabteca. The sons of Raama were Sheba and Dedan.
0:8.note: This list echoes the Table of Nations in Genesis 10, quietly tracing how all peoples descend from Noah and belong to God's story.

0:9.rendering: Cush fathered Nimrod. He was the first to become a mighty one on the earth.
0:9.note: Nimrod's name in Hebrew plays on "rebel," marking him as the Bible's original empire builder right after the flood.

0:10.rendering: Mizraim fathered the Ludites, the Anamites, the Lehabites, the Naphtuhites,
0:10.note: This list of Mizraim's sons traces the ancient Egyptians' neighbors and kin, echoing the Table of Nations in Genesis 10 with slight name variations that reflect how oral traditions shifted across centuries.

0:11.rendering: Pathrusim, Casluhim (from whom the Philistines came), and Caphtorim.
0:11.note: The Philistines trace their roots to Casluhim, a detail that echoes the Table of Nations in Genesis 10:14 and quietly hints at how even Israel's fiercest enemies fit inside God's ancient map of humanity.

0:12.rendering: Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn, and Heth,
0:12.note: This terse genealogy echoes Genesis 10:15, linking Israel's story to the ancient Canaanite peoples through one shared ancestral table.

0:13.rendering: the Jebusites, the Amorites, the Girgashites,
0:13.note: This list of seven Canaanite peoples echoes the repeated ancient warning in Deuteronomy 7:1 that Israel must not assimilate with them.

0:14.rendering: And the Hivites, and the Arkites, and the Sinites,
0:14.note: This list of Canaanite clans echoes Genesis 10:17, stitching the Chronicler's genealogy back to the table of nations after the flood.

0:15.rendering: and the Aramean, and the Ashurite, and the Letushite, and the Kepharite,
0:15.note: This list of Aramean clans echoes the table of nations in Genesis 10, quietly stitching Chronicles back into the ancient roots of all peoples.

1:8.rendering: The sons of Hezron who were born to him were Jerahmeel, Ram, and Chelubai.
1:8.note: Hezron's three sons launch the three major branches of Judah's clan, setting up the sprawling family tree that will eventually spotlight David and the royal line.

1:9.rendering: Ram fathered Amminadab, and Amminadab fathered Nahshon, leader of the sons of Judah.
1:9.note: This genealogy quietly threads the royal line from Judah through Nahshon, the very man who led the tribe out of Egypt, echoing the promise that kings would come from Judah's descendants.

1:10.rendering: Nahshon fathered Salmon, Salmon fathered Boaz.
1:10.note: This quiet link in David's genealogy echoes the story of redemption in Ruth, where Boaz the redeemer enters the family line from the tribe of Judah.

1:11.rendering: Boaz fathered Obed, and Obed fathered Jesse.
1:11.note: This link in David's genealogy quietly echoes the book of Ruth, where Boaz and Ruth's son Obed becomes the grandfather of Israel's greatest king.

1:12.rendering: Jesse fathered Eliab his firstborn, Abinadab the second, Shimea the third,
1:12.note: Jesse's family list quietly echoes the promise that a king would come from Judah's line, setting the stage for David.

1:13.rendering: Nethanel the fourth, Raddai the fifth,
1:13.note: The Chronicler weaves these names into Judah's family tree, quietly tracing the royal line that will one day lead to David and beyond.

1:14.rendering: Jesse fathered Eliab his firstborn, Abinadab the second, Shimea the third, Nethanel the fourth, Raddai the fifth, Ozem the sixth, David the seventh.
1:14.note: The seventh son slot echoes the creation week, quietly signaling that from this overlooked family line a new royal order is about to dawn.

1:15.rendering: Their sisters were Zeruiah and Abigail. The sons of Zeruiah were Abishai, Joab, and Asahel, three in all.
1:15.note: These sisters link David to his formidable nephews, the commanders who shaped his rise, echoing how family webs drive the unified story of Israel's kings.

0:16.rendering: The sons of Shem were Elam, Asshur, Arphaxad, Lud, and Aram. The sons of Aram were Uz, Hul, Gether, and Meshech.
0:16.note: This genealogy links the line of Shem straight back to Noah, quietly tracing the ancient roots of Israel among the nations.

0:17.rendering: Arphaxad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber.
0:17.note: This sparse genealogy quietly stitches the post-flood world to the line that will produce Abraham, echoing the Table of Nations in Genesis 10-11.

0:18.rendering: To Eber were born two sons: the name of the first was Peleg, for in his days the earth was divided, and the name of his brother was Joktan.
0:18.note: The name Peleg echoes the Hebrew verb for "division," hinting at the scattering of nations after Babel in Genesis 11.

0:19.rendering: Joktan fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah,
0:19.note: This list of Joktan's sons echoes the ancient Arabian tribes, mapping the spread of Semitic peoples in the post-flood world.

0:20.note: This terse genealogy echoes the Table of Nations in Genesis 10:27, quietly mapping how all peoples descend from Noah's sons.

0:21.note: This tight genealogy links back to Shem's line in Genesis 10, tracing the ancient roots of the peoples Israel would later encounter.

0:22.note: This snippet closes a list of Joktan's thirteen sons, whose names echo ancient Arabian tribes and trading posts along the spice routes.

0:23.note: This bare list of names quietly echoes the flood's reset, tracing the one unbroken line of promise from Noah through Shem.

1:16.rendering: Abigail gave birth to Amasa, and the father of Amasa was Jether the Ishmaelite.
1:16.note: The detail that Amasa's father was an Ishmaelite hints at unexpected alliances in David's family tree, echoing how God weaves outsiders into his people's story.

1:17.rendering: Caleb son of Hezron fathered sons by Azubah his wife, and by Jerioth. These were her sons: Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon.
1:17.note: This tangled genealogy of Caleb's three wives and multiple sons quietly threads the promise of land and fruitfulness from Numbers 13-14 into David's family tree.

1:18.rendering: When Azubah died, Caleb took Ephrath as a wife, and she bore him Hur.
1:18.note: Ephrath links back to the ancient name for Bethlehem, quietly seeding the future hometown of David and the Messiah through this family line.

1:19.rendering: Hur fathered Uri, and Uri fathered Bezalel.
1:19.note: Bezalel, the master craftsman of the tabernacle, traces his line back through this genealogy to Judah, weaving artistry into the messianic family tree.

1:20.rendering: Later Hezron went in to the daughter of Machir the father of Gilead. He took her when he was sixty years old, and she bore him Segub.
1:20.note: Hezron fathers a new line at sixty, echoing Abraham's late-life miracle and launching the Gileadite branch that will shape Israel's eastern tribes.

1:21.rendering: Segub fathered Jair. He held twenty-three towns in the land of Gilead.
1:21.note: The name Jair echoes the earlier judge who captured these same Gilead towns, linking this genealogy to the heroic exploits in Judges 10.

1:22.rendering: But Geshur and Aram took from them the villages of Jair, Kenath and its surrounding settlements, sixty towns. All these were descendants of Makir, father of Gilead.
1:22.note: Sixty towns captured by neighboring Arameans echoes the conquest language of Joshua, showing how even the mighty clans of Manasseh lost ground in the land.

1:23.rendering: After Hezron died in Caleb-ephrathah, Abijah, Hezron's wife, bore him Ashhur the father of Tekoa.
1:23.note: This terse genealogy quietly weaves Hezron's line into Judah's heartland through a posthumous son born in Caleb's territory.

0:24.note: These names form the spine of the post-flood genealogy, echoing the tower-of-Babel dispersion in Genesis 10-11.

0:25.rendering: Abraham fathered Isaac. Isaac fathered Jacob and Esau.
0:25.note: This single verse stitches the patriarchal line into the vast web of nations from chapter 1, showing how the family that will become Israel emerges from the whole human family.

0:26.note: The name change from Abram to Abraham echoes God's covenant promise to make him the father of many nations, a thread that runs through Genesis 17 and shapes Israel's identity.

0:27.rendering: The sons of Abraham were Isaac and Ishmael.
0:27.note: This terse family ledger quietly sets up the entire biblical drama of promise passing through Isaac, not the firstborn Ishmael.

0:28.rendering: These are their generations: the firstborn of Ishmael was Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,
0:28.note: This list of Ishmael's sons echoes the promise in Genesis 17 that he would become a great nation, twelve princes from one father.

0:29.rendering: Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.
0:29.note: These names trace Ishmael's desert clans, echoing the promise in Genesis 17 that he would father twelve rulers, a subtle thread of divine faithfulness to Abraham's other line.

0:30.rendering: Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah. These were the sons of Ishmael.
0:30.note: This list echoes the genealogy in Genesis 25:13-16, preserving the ancient tribal memory of Ishmael's twelve sons as desert peoples.

0:31.rendering: The sons of Keturah, Abraham's concubine: she bore Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah. The sons of Jokshan: Sheba and Dedan.
0:31.note: Keturah's name means "incense," linking her line to the aromatic trade routes of Sheba and Dedan, ancient caravan hubs.

1:24.rendering: The sons of Jerahmeel the firstborn of Hezron were Ram the firstborn, Bunah, Oren, Ozem, and Ahijah.
1:24.note: Jerahmeel, whose name means "God is compassionate," heads the first clan list in Judah's genealogy, quietly signaling that the royal tribe begins with mercy rather than might.

1:25.rendering: Jerahmeel also had another wife, whose name was Atarah. She was the mother of Onam.
1:25.note: The Hebrew highlights yet another wife branching the family tree, showing how ancient genealogies wove complex kinship networks together.

1:26.rendering: The sons of Ram, the firstborn of Jerahmeel, were Maaz, Jamin, and Eker.
1:26.note: Ram means "high one," echoing the elevated line of Judah's clan through Jerahmeel's firstborn.

1:27.rendering: The sons of Onam were Shammai and Jada. The sons of Shammai were Nadab and Abishur.
1:27.note: This bare list of names quietly stitches the tribe of Judah into a vast family tree that will one day include King David.

1:28.rendering: And the name of the wife of Abishur was Abihail, and she bore him Ahban and Molid.
1:28.note: This quiet genealogy note links two otherwise unknown names to the sprawling Judahite family tree, showing how the Chronicler stitches even minor branches into the story of God's people.

1:29.rendering: The sons of Nadab were Seled and Appaim. Seled died without children.
1:29.note: A quiet reminder that not every family branch flourishes, an ancient echo of how inheritance lines can simply end.

1:30.rendering: The sons of Appaim: Ishi. The sons of Ishi: Sheshan. The sons of Sheshan: Ahlai.
1:30.note: This terse genealogy quietly threads the promise of descendants through a family line that nearly dies out.

1:31.rendering: The sons of Jada, Shammai's brother: Jether and Jonathan. Jether died without children.
1:31.note: This short notice of a childless death quietly echoes the ancestral worry over an endangered family line that runs from Genesis onward.

0:32.rendering: The sons of Midian were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the sons of Keturah.
0:32.note: These five names echo the earlier genealogy of Ishmael, quietly showing how Abraham's line branches into multiple nomadic peoples.

0:33.rendering: Abraham fathered Isaac. The sons of Isaac were Esau and Israel.
0:33.note: This single verse compresses the entire patriarchal drama, pivoting from Abraham's line to the fateful twins whose rivalry will shape Israel's story.

0:34.rendering: The sons of Esau were Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
0:34.note: These five names echo the earlier genealogy of Esau in Genesis 36, stitching the Chronicler's list firmly to Israel's foundational family story.

0:35.rendering: The sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, Kenaz, Timna, and Amalek.
0:35.note: Timna appears here as a son of Eliphaz, yet in Genesis 36 she is his concubine who bore Amalek, a subtle shift that highlights how genealogies weave together family and tribal identities.

0:36.rendering: The sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah.
0:36.note: This list of Reuel's sons echoes the earlier genealogy in Genesis 36:13, quietly stitching the chronicler's family tree back to the ancient Esau traditions.

0:37.rendering: The sons of Seir: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan.
0:37.note: Seir's sons here link Esau's family to the ancient inhabitants of the land that would become Edom, an unexpected thread of kinship in the Bible's family tree.

0:38.rendering: The sons of Lotan: Hori and Homam. Timna was Lotan's sister.
0:38.note: Lotan's sister Timna appears here as a named woman in a genealogy, an unusual detail that echoes later when a Timna becomes mother to Amalek in Esau's line.

0:39.rendering: The sons of Shobal: Alian, Manahath, Ebal, Shephi, and Onam. The sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah.
0:39.note: This list of Edomite clan leaders echoes the genealogy in Genesis 36, linking Israel's neighbor to the family of Esau and showing how the Chronicler weaves older traditions into his unified story of God's people.

1:32.rendering: The sons of Jonathan were Peleth and Zaza. These were the descendants of Jerahmeel.
1:32.note: This terse genealogy quietly weaves the obscure line of Jonathan and Jerahmeel into the sprawling family tree of Judah.

1:33.rendering: Sheshan had no sons, only daughters. He had an Egyptian slave named Jarha.
1:33.note: An Egyptian slave becomes family heir through marriage, quietly showing how God's people already included outsiders long before the exile.

1:34.rendering: Sheshan had no sons, only daughters. He had an Egyptian slave named Jarha, and he gave his daughter to Jarha his slave as a wife, and she bore him Attai.
1:34.note: An Egyptian slave marrying into an Israelite clan shows how the family tree of Judah welcomed outsiders, echoing God's promise that all nations would be blessed through Abraham's line.

1:35.rendering: Attai fathered Nathan, and Nathan fathered Zabad.
1:35.note: This link in David's family tree quietly carries the royal line forward, one ordinary father-son step at a time.

1:36.rendering: Zabad fathered Ephlal, Ephlal fathered Obed,
1:36.note: This quiet link in David's family tree echoes the name Obed from Ruth's story, tying Judah's line together across generations.

1:37.rendering: Obed fathered Jehu, and Jehu fathered Azariah.
1:37.note: This link in the family tree quietly bridges two otherwise unrelated stories in Chronicles, tying a minor Judahite line into the larger tapestry of David's descendants.

1:38.rendering: Zaza fathered Aglai, and Aglai fathered Sheshan.
1:38.note: This link in the Judahite genealogy quietly bridges a family line that will later produce the remarkable daughters of Sheshan.

1:39.rendering: Eleazar fathered Phinehas, Phinehas fathered Abishua,
1:39.note: This quiet link in the Judahite chain echoes the priestly line from Exodus and Numbers, showing how royal and sacred families intertwined in ancient Israel.

1:40.rendering: Shallum fathered Jekamiah, and Jekamiah fathered Elishama.
1:40.note: This quiet link in Judah's genealogy quietly carries the royal line forward toward the kings and ultimately the Messiah.

1:41.rendering: The sons of Caleb the brother of Jerahmeel were Mesha his firstborn, who was the father of Ziph, and the sons of Mareshah the father of Hebron.
1:41.note: Caleb's line weaves through Judah's genealogy like a branching vine, linking key towns such as Ziph and Hebron to the clan's ancient roots.

1:42.rendering: The sons of Hebron were Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema.
1:42.note: This terse genealogy of Hebron's sons echoes the ancient practice of listing clans to show how Judah's territory and identity grew through family lines.

1:43.rendering: Shema fathered Raham, the father of Jorkeam. Rekem fathered Shammai.
1:43.note: This snippet of names continues the Chronicler's careful weaving of Judah's clan tree, echoing the earlier promise that a ruler would come from Judah's line.

1:44.rendering: The son of Shammai was Maon, and Maon was the father of Beth-zur.
1:44.note: This short link in Judah's sprawling family tree quietly anchors a town that later became a key Judean stronghold.

1:45.rendering: Ephah, Caleb's concubine, bore Haran, Moza, and Gazez. Haran fathered Gazez.
1:45.note: This terse genealogy quietly reveals how Caleb's family expanded through a secondary wife, echoing the ancient practice of concubinage to secure heirs and land.

1:46.rendering: The sons of Jahdai were Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph.
1:46.note: This list of obscure names anchors a larger Judahite genealogy, reminding us the Bible treasures every branch of the family tree.

1:47.rendering: Maacah, Caleb's concubine, bore Sheber and Tirzah.
1:47.note: This brief list of Caleb's descendants through his concubine Maacah quietly weaves the Calebite clan into Judah's sprawling family tree.

0:40.rendering: The sons of Anah: Dishon. The sons of Dishon: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
0:40.note: Anah's line branches into four clans through Dishon, preserving the detailed genealogy of Edomite tribes in the ancient Near East.

0:41.rendering: The descendants of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan. The descendants of Dishan: Uz and Aran.
0:41.note: This short genealogy links Edom's clans to the line of Esau, showing how the Bible weaves neighboring peoples into the single family story that will eventually reach Jesus.

0:42.rendering: These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the sons of Israel: Bela son of Beor, and the name of his city was Dinhabah.
0:42.note: This list of Edomite kings quietly echoes the promise to Abraham that kings would come from his line, yet here they rise in the land of his rival Esau first.

0:43.rendering: When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned in his place.
0:43.note: Bozrah was a key Edomite stronghold, and this king-list quietly echoes the ancient rivalry between Esau's line and Jacob's that runs from Genesis to the prophets.

0:44.rendering: Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned in his place, and when Jobab died, Husham from the land of the Temanites reigned in his place.
0:44.note: This king list quietly echoes the ancient rivalry between Edom and Israel, with Bozrah later becoming a prophetic symbol of divine judgment in Isaiah and Jeremiah.

0:45.rendering: When Husham died, Hadad son of Bedad, who defeated Midian in the country of Moab, became king in his place. The name of his city was Avith.
0:45.note: Hadad's victory over Midian in Moab echoes Israel's own battles with the same foes, showing how God raises deliverers among surrounding nations too.

0:46.rendering: Hadad died, and Samlah from Masrekah ruled in his place.
0:46.note: This rapid-fire king list echoes the ancient Near Eastern genre of royal genealogies, quietly showing how power shifts like sand in Edom's deserts.

0:47.rendering: When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah became king in his place.
0:47.note: This genealogy thread links Edom's kings to the line of Esau, showing how the promised land's neighbors were once family.

1:48.rendering: She also bore Shaaph the father of Madmannah, Sheva the father of Machbenah, and the father of Gibea. And the daughter of Caleb was Achsah.
1:48.note: Caleb's line quietly weaves new towns and a bold daughter into Israel's story, echoing how one faithful scout's legacy keeps expanding.

1:49.rendering: The sons of Caleb the son of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah: Shobal the father of Kiriath-jearim,
1:49.note: Caleb's line through Hur links Judah's clan to the ancient city of Kiriath-jearim, where the ark later rested.

1:50.rendering: Salma father of Bethlehem, Hareph father of Beth-gader.
1:50.note: This terse genealogy quietly anchors the line of David, since Bethlehem will soon birth Israel's greatest king.

1:51.rendering: The sons of Shobal were Haroeh, and half of the Manahathites.
1:51.note: Shobal's line splits into Haroeh and half the Manahathites, hinting at how ancient clans often divided land and identity.

1:52.rendering: The families of Kiriath-jearim: the Ithrites, the Puhites, the Shumathites, and the Mishraites. From these came the Zorathites and the Eshtaolites.
1:52.note: These names trace the clans that branched out from Kiriath-jearim, preserving memory of how one town seeded whole new settlements in Judah.

1:53.rendering: The sons of Salma: Bethlehem, the Netophathites, Atroth-beth-joab, and half the Manahathites, the Zorites.
1:53.note: Salma's line quietly anchors Bethlehem as a clan hub, an ancient echo that will later cradle David's family and a future king.

1:54.rendering: The clans of scribes who lived at Jabez were the Tirathites, the Shimeathites, and the Sucathites. These are the Kenites who came from Hammath, the father of the house of Rechab.
1:54.note: These scribe clans trace their roots to the ancient Kenites, nomadic metalworkers linked to Moses' in-laws, showing how outsiders became woven into Israel's story.

0:48.rendering: When Shaul died, Baal-hanan son of Achbor reigned in his place.
0:48.note: This brief king list quietly echoes the earlier Edomite genealogy, reminding us that even royal lines rise and fall like any other.

0:49.rendering: When Baal-hanan son of Achbor died, Hadad succeeded him as king. His city was Pai, and his wife's name was Mehetabel daughter of Matred daughter of Mezahab.
0:49.note: This list of Edomite kings quietly foreshadows Israel's own monarchy, showing God sovereignly shaping even rival nations long before David.

0:50.rendering: Hadad died, and the chiefs of Edom were: chief Timna, chief Alvah, chief Jetheth,
0:50.note: These "chiefs" (alluphim) echo the earlier list of Esau's descendants, showing how family lines hardened into political power structures in Edom.

0:51.rendering: Duke, the chief of the Temanites, and Duke, the chief of the Aholibamites, and Duke, the chief of the Maonites.
0:51.note: This list of Edomite dukes echoes the earlier Genesis 36 genealogy, preserving the same ancient tribal structure of Esau's descendants.

0:52.rendering: Chief Teman, chief Omar, chief Zepho, chief Kenaz,
0:52.note: These "chiefs" translate the Hebrew word for clan leaders, painting Edom as a network of powerful family strongholds rather than a single kingdom.

0:53.rendering: The chiefs of Timna, Alvah, Jetheth,
0:53.note: These Edomite clan leaders close the genealogy, echoing the earlier list of Esau's descendants and showing how one family tree branched into nations.

2:0.rendering: These were the sons of David who were born to him in Hebron: the firstborn Amnon by Ahinoam the Jezreelite, the second Daniel by Abigail the Carmelite,
2:0.note: The careful ordering of David's sons by birth and mother quietly traces the fractured family lines that will shape the rest of the kingdom's story.

2:1.rendering: The third, Absalom the son of Maacah the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur; the fourth, Adonijah the son of Haggith;
2:1.note: This list quietly echoes the family fractures that will tear David's house apart, with Absalom's foreign mother hinting at future rebellion.

2:2.rendering: His third, Absalom the son of Maacah the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur; the fourth, Adonijah the son of Haggith;
2:2.note: This list quietly echoes David's fractured family life, with Absalom's mother linking him to a foreign king's daughter and the seeds of future rebellion.

2:3.rendering: These six were born to him in Hebron. He reigned there seven years and six months. Then in Jerusalem he reigned thirty-three years over all Israel and Judah.
2:3.note: The precise numbers, seven years and six months in Hebron followed by thirty-three in Jerusalem, echo the exact chronology given in 2 Samuel 5:5, linking the Chronicler's royal genealogy back to the older narrative.

2:4.rendering: And these were born to David in Jerusalem: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon, four sons by Bathsheba daughter of Ammiel.
2:4.note: The Hebrew lists Bathsheba not by her famous name but as "Bath-shua daughter of Ammiel," echoing the earlier Tamar story while quietly linking this royal line to the woman at the center of David's greatest failure.

2:5.note: This list of David's sons born in Jerusalem echoes the royal line leading to the Messiah, quietly stitching the Chronicler's genealogy to the promise of an enduring throne.

2:6.note: These three names close a list of David's sons born in Jerusalem, echoing the earlier births in Hebron and hinting at the expanding royal household amid political turbulence.

2:7.note: These three names close a list of David's Jerusalem-born sons, echoing the hopeful "God has heard" and "God knows" themes that run through the royal line.

3:0.rendering: The sons of Judah: Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal.
3:0.note: This opening list restarts the Judah genealogy from Genesis 38 and 46, weaving family lines that will later birth Israel's greatest king.

3:1.rendering: Reaiah son of Shobal fathered Jahath, and Jahath fathered Ahumai and Lahad. These were the clans of the Zorathites.
3:1.note: This genealogy quietly links the obscure Zorathites back to Judah's line through Shobal, one of Caleb's descendants, showing how even small clans fit into the larger tribal story.

3:2.rendering: These were the sons of Etam: Jezreel, Ishma, and Idbash, and their sister was named Hazzelelponi.
3:2.note: This brief genealogy from Judah's line preserves the name of a woman, Hazzelelponi, whose very mention hints at the quiet importance of daughters in ancient family memory.

3:3.rendering: Penuel was the father of Gedor, and Ezer the father of Hushah. These were the sons of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah, the father of Bethlehem.
3:3.note: This list quietly threads back to the famous clan of Hur, linking obscure Judahite villages to the very family that birthed Bethlehem itself.

3:4.rendering: Ashhur father of Tekoa had two wives: Helah and Naarah.
3:4.note: The name Ashhur plays on the Hebrew word for "black" or "dark," while his wives' names mean "rust" and "young woman," hinting at wordplay typical of these genealogical lists.

3:5.rendering: Naarah gave birth for him to Ahuzam, Hepher, Temeni, and Haahashtari. These were the sons of Naarah.
3:5.note: The name Haahashtari literally means "the one of the courtyard," an unusual tag that may hint at Persian-era influence on this Judahite clan list.

3:6.rendering: And the sons of Helah were Zereth, Zohar, Ethnan,
3:6.note: Helah's name means "rust" or "sickness," a surprising detail tucked into a genealogy that hints at how even flawed family lines weave into Israel's story.

3:7.rendering: Koz fathered Anub, Zobebah, and the clans of Aharhel son of Harum.
3:7.note: The name Koz means "thorn bush" in Hebrew, a surprising detail that echoes the wilderness imagery threading through Israel's family lists.

2:8.rendering: All these were the sons of David, besides the sons of the concubines. Tamar was their sister.
2:8.note: This list quietly echoes Genesis 38, where Tamar's story first shattered family lines, now woven into David's royal tree.

2:9.rendering: The descendants of Solomon were Rehoboam, Abijah his son, Asa his son, Jehoshaphat his son,
2:9.note: This king list quietly echoes the covenant promise to David in 2 Samuel 7, tracing the royal line that will one day lead to the Messiah.

2:10.rendering: Azariah fathered Hezekiah, Hezekiah fathered Manasseh, Manasseh fathered Amon, Amon fathered Josiah.
2:10.note: This royal line from Hezekiah to Josiah echoes the promise to David while carrying the weight of Judah's coming exile.

2:11.rendering: Amaziah fathered Azariah, Azariah fathered Jotham.
2:11.note: This quiet link in David's royal line quietly echoes the promise of an enduring house, bridging the fractured kingdom toward the coming Messiah.

2:12.rendering: Ahaz his son, Hezekiah his son, Manasseh his son,
2:12.note: This single line quietly stitches three kings together, each one a living echo of God's promise to David that his house would endure forever.

2:13.rendering: Amon his son, Josiah his son.
2:13.note: This terse genealogy thread quietly carries the Davidic line through the darkest years of Judah's decline, right up to the reformer king Josiah.

2:14.rendering: The sons of Josiah: Johanan the firstborn, Jehoiakim the second, Zedekiah the third, Shallum the fourth.
2:14.note: This list quietly traces the final kings of Judah, whose reigns spiral toward exile.

2:15.rendering: The sons of Josiah: Johanan his firstborn, Jehoiakim the second, Zedekiah the third, Shallum the fourth.
2:15.note: This list quietly traces the royal line through the final kings of Judah, ending in exile.

3:8.rendering: Jabez was more honorable than his brothers. His mother named him Jabez, saying, "Because I bore him in pain."
3:8.note: The name Jabez sounds like the Hebrew word for "pain," turning a mother's sorrow into a memorable label that this man would later redeem through bold prayer.

3:9.rendering: Jabez called out to the God of Israel, "If only you would bless me indeed, enlarge my border, let your hand be with me, and keep me from harm so that it does not bring me pain." God granted what he asked.
3:9.note: The name Jabez means "he causes pain," yet his bold prayer reverses that very curse through four simple pleas rooted in the ancient blessing of the land.

3:10.rendering: Kelub brother of Shuhah fathered Mehir, who fathered Eshton.
3:10.note: This brief genealogy from Judah's line quietly preserves family threads that the Chronicler weaves into Israel's larger story of restoration.

3:11.rendering: Tehinnah fathered Ir-Nahash, who fathered Eshton. Eshton fathered Beth-Rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah father of Ir-Nahash. These are the men of Recah.
3:11.note: This list of Judah's descendants includes a rare name meaning "supplication" or "prayer," hinting at how even obscure family lines in the Chronicler's genealogy carry echoes of dependence on God.

3:12.rendering: The sons of Kenaz: Othniel and Seraiah. The sons of Othniel: Hathath and Meonothai.
3:12.note: This brief genealogy quietly introduces Othniel, the first judge who delivered Israel from oppression, echoing the earlier spy narrative in Joshua where his bravery won him land.

3:13.rendering: Meonothai fathered Ophrah, and Seraiah fathered Joab father of Ge-harashim, for they were craftsmen.
3:13.note: The name Ge-harashim means "valley of craftsmen," a place-name that echoes how family trades shaped entire communities in ancient Judah.

3:14.rendering: The sons of Caleb the son of Jephunneh: Iru, Elah, and Naam. And the sons of Elah: Kenaz.
3:14.note: This scrap of genealogy quietly honors Caleb, the faithful spy who wholly followed the Lord, linking his line to later Judahite clans.

3:15.rendering: The sons of Jehallelel were Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel.
3:15.note: This brief list of Jehallelel's sons sits quietly in a Judahite genealogy, preserving names that echo like forgotten whispers from Israel's ancient family tree.

2:16.rendering: The sons of Jeconiah the captive: Shealtiel his son,
2:16.note: Jeconiah's nickname "the captive" echoes the trauma of 597 BC exile, linking this royal line to the shattered throne of David.

2:17.rendering: And the sons of Jeconiah the captive: Shealtiel his son,
2:17.note: Jeconiah's label as "the captive" echoes his humiliating exile in Babylon, a painful family memory preserved in the royal genealogy.

2:18.rendering: The sons of Pedaiah: Zerubbabel and Shimei. The sons of Zerubbabel: Meshullam and Hananiah, and Shelomith was their sister.
2:18.note: Zerubbabel, the post-exile governor and temple rebuilder, appears here in David's family tree, quietly linking the royal line to the return from Babylon.

2:19.rendering: And the sons of Elioenai: Hodaviah, Eliashib, Pelaiah, Akkub, Johanan, Delaiah, and Anani, seven sons.
2:19.note: Elioenai's seven sons close the Davidic genealogy in 1 Chronicles, their names evoking echoes of restoration and divine favor in the post-exile context.

2:20.rendering: The sons of Hananiah: Pelatiah and Jeshaiah, and the sons of Jeshaiah: Rephaiah and Arnan, Obadiah and Shecaniah.
2:20.note: This genealogy traces the line of David through the exiled king Jehoiachin, quietly preserving the royal seed in Babylon after the fall of Jerusalem.

2:21.rendering: The sons of Shecaniah: Shemaiah. The sons of Shemaiah: Hattush, Igal, Bariah, Neariah, and Shaphat, six in all.
2:21.note: This list quietly traces the line of David through the exile, preserving hope for a future king.

2:22.rendering: The sons of Neariah: Elioenai, Hizkiah, and Azrikam, three.
2:22.note: This terse list quietly echoes the royal line's narrowing after exile, preserving names that link David's house to the postexilic community.

2:23.rendering: The sons of Elioenai: Hodaviah, Eliashib, Pelaiah, Akkub, Johanan, Delaiah, and Anani, seven in all.
2:23.note: This list of seven sons closes the royal line from David in the post-exile genealogy, quietly preserving the hope of a future anointed one from David's house.

3:16.rendering: The sons of Ezrah: Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon. Mered took a wife from Egypt, and she bore Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah the father of Eshtemoa.
3:16.note: Ezrah's family tree suddenly spotlights a foreign Egyptian wife, quietly echoing how outsiders weave into Israel's story from the beginning.

3:17.rendering: His Judahite wife gave birth to Jered the father of Gedor, Heber the father of Soco, and Jekuthiel the father of Zanoah.
3:17.note: This brief genealogy quietly weaves a Judahite woman into the Egyptian line of Mered, showing how the tribes interlocked like threads in a single tapestry.

3:18.rendering: The sons of the wife of Hodiah, the sister of Naham, were the father of Keilah the Garmite and Eshtemoa the Maacathite.
3:18.note: This brief genealogy quietly weaves together Judahite clans with foreign-sounding names, hinting at Israel's porous borders and surprising family ties in the ancient tribal landscape.

3:19.rendering: The sons of Shimon were Amnon, Rinnah, Ben-hanan, and Tilon. The sons of Ishi were Zoheth and Ben-zoheth.
3:19.note: This list of Simeon's obscure descendants quietly preserves the tribe's lineage in the post-exile community, echoing how God keeps track of every branch in the family tree.

3:20.rendering: The sons of Shelah son of Judah were Er the father of Lecah, Laadah the father of Mareshah, and the clans of the linen workers at Beth-ashbea.
3:20.note: Shelah's line quietly preserves a specialized craft guild of linen workers, an echo of Judah's ancient textile trade in the southern hills.

3:21.rendering: and Jokim, and the men of Cozeba, and Joash, and Saraph, who ruled in Moab and returned to Lehem. Now these are ancient matters.
3:21.note: The phrase "ancient matters" signals these are old, half-forgotten family traditions worth preserving in the Chronicler's genealogy.

3:22.rendering: These were the potters who lived at Netaim and Gederah. They lived there with the king for his work. They stayed in his service.
3:22.note: These potters served the king in his royal workshops, an echo of how everyday crafts supported the house of David.

3:23.rendering: The sons of Simeon: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul.
3:23.note: This list echoes the earlier census in Numbers 26:12-14, showing how Simeon's clan lines persisted from wilderness wanderings into the chronicler's day.

3:24.rendering: Shallum was his son, Mibsam his son, Mishma his son.
3:24.note: This quiet link in the Simeon genealogy quietly echoes the promise of multiplied descendants from Abraham.

3:25.rendering: And the sons of Mishma: Hammuel his son, Zaccur his son, and Shimei his son.
3:25.note: This snippet from the sprawling Judahite genealogy quietly preserves family memory across generations, echoing how the Bible stitches thousands of ordinary names into the larger story of God's people.

3:26.rendering: Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters, but his brothers did not have many children, so all their clans did not multiply like the sons of Judah.
3:26.note: The Hebrew word for "multiply" echoes God's original command to be fruitful, highlighting how most clans fell short of Judah's explosive growth.

3:27.rendering: They lived in Beersheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual,
3:27.note: This list of towns echoes the earlier tribal allotments in Joshua 19:2-6, showing how the chronicler weaves older land promises into Judah's ongoing story.

3:28.rendering: Bilhan, Ezer, and Dishan. These were the sons of Seir.
3:28.note: This snippet of names links Edom's ancient clan list to the Horite inhabitants of Seir, showing how Israel's neighbors were woven into the same genealogical web as Abraham's family.

3:29.rendering: And at Bethuel, and at Hormah, and at Ziklag.
3:29.note: These three towns sit in the arid southern borderlands, echoing the same list of Simeonite settlements found in Joshua 19, where the tribe received its inheritance by lot.

3:30.rendering: Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susim, Beth-biri, and Shaaraim. These were their cities until the reign of David.
3:30.note: The list of towns ends with a quiet time marker, until David, hinting how Judah's inheritance story is about to shift under the coming king.

3:31.rendering: Their villages were Etam, Ain, Rimmon, Token, and Ashan, five towns,
3:31.note: This list of five Judahite villages quietly echoes the earlier allotment of towns to Simeon in Joshua 19, showing how the Chronicler stitches old tribal boundaries into his vision of restored Israel.

3:32.rendering: These were their settlements, and they kept their own genealogical records.
3:32.note: This verse anchors the Simeonite list in real places and family trees, showing how identity and land were woven together in ancient Israel.

3:33.rendering: Meshobab, Jamlech, Joshah son of Amaziah,
3:33.note: This snippet sits in a long list of Simeonite clan leaders, quietly showing how the once-nomadic tribe of Simeon carved out settled territory in the south.

3:34.rendering: and Joel, and Jehu the son of Joshibiah, son of Seraiah, son of Asiel,
3:34.note: This snippet from Simeon's genealogy quietly echoes the promise that a great nation would come from Abraham's descendants, even the overlooked tribes.

3:35.rendering: And Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, and Benaiah,
3:35.note: These seven names close a surprising list of Simeonite clans that expanded south into Edomite territory during Hezekiah's reign.

3:36.rendering: And Ziza the son of Shiphi, son of Allon, son of Jedaiah, son of Shimri, son of Shemaiah,
3:36.note: This link in a long Judahite genealogy quietly carries forward the royal tribe's story from Genesis 49 into the post-exile community.

3:37.rendering: These mentioned by name were leaders in their clans, and their ancestral households increased greatly.
3:37.note: The phrase "mentioned by name" echoes the book's opening genealogies, highlighting how obscure clan leaders became noteworthy simply because their households multiplied under God's ancient promise to Abraham.

3:38.rendering: They went to the entrance of Gedor, as far as the east side of the valley, to seek pasture for their flocks.
3:38.note: This quiet migration notice echoes the ancestral promise of land and increase, as the sons of Simeon push eastward like Abraham once did.

3:39.rendering: They found rich, good pasture, and the land was wide open, quiet, and peaceful, for the former inhabitants were from Ham.
3:39.note: The phrase "quiet and peaceful" echoes the ideal rest promised in the land, a rare moment of shalom amid the chronicler's tribal lists.

3:40.rendering: These men, whose names are written above, came in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah. They attacked the tents of the Meunites and the people who were found there, they devoted them to destruction, and they settled in their place to this day. For there was pasture for their flocks.
3:40.note: This verse echoes the conquest language of Joshua, showing how even in the divided kingdom era, Judah's clans reenact ancient patterns of claiming land through holy war.

3:41.rendering: And some of them from the sons of Simeon, five hundred men, went to Mount Seir. Their leaders were Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel, the sons of Ishi.
3:41.note: These Simeonites carve out new territory in Edomite land, echoing how their ancestor Jacob once wrestled for blessing at a place called Peniel.

3:42.rendering: They struck down the remnant of the Amalekites who had escaped, and they have lived there to this day.
3:42.note: The phrase "to this day" anchors this conquest in the Chronicler's own time, showing how one tribe's victory over ancient foes still echoes generations later.
